queue.title: 1a: Translate verbal descriptions into correct numerical expressions using operation symbols and grouping
1c: Translate between algebraic expressions and verbal expressions using correct mathematical terminology
1e: Identify and apply the reflexive, symmetric, and transitive properties of equality
1g: Apply the Distributive Property to rewrite and evaluate numerical expressions, including mixed numbers and decimals
1i: Write absolute value expressions that model real-world distances and accuracy as positive differences
1k: Use ratios and percentages as metrics to model real-world situations and make decisions
10b: Differentiate between synthetic geometry and analytic geometry based on their defining characteristics
10a: Understand the structure of an axiomatic system and distinguish between axioms and conclusions
10d: Draw and label figures that accurately represent given geometric relationships
10c: Identify and name points, lines, and planes from geometric figures
10f: Apply the Segment Addition Postulate to find missing segment lengths
10e: Find the length of a segment by reading coordinates from a number line
10h: Apply the Distance Formula to solve real-world problems
10g: Use the Distance Formula to find the distance between two points in the coordinate plane
10i: Find the coordinate of a point located at a fractional distance between two given points on a number line
10j: Locate a point that divides a segment on a number line in a given ratio
10k: Find the coordinates of a point that divides a segment in a given ratio on a coordinate plane
10m: Find the coordinates of a missing endpoint when the midpoint and one endpoint are known
10l: Use the Midpoint Formula to find the coordinates of the midpoint of a segment in the coordinate plane
11b: Apply the Angle Addition Postulate and the definition of an angle bisector to find unknown angle measures
11a: Identify and name the parts of an angle, including the vertex and sides
11c: Solve problems involving complementary, supplementary, and vertical angle relationships using algebra
11d: Use the fact that perpendicular lines form right angles to write and solve equations for unknown angle measures
11f: Apply coordinate geometry to identify figures from vertices and compute their measurements
11e: Calculate the perimeter, circumference, and area of two-dimensional figures from coordinate grids and labeled diagrams
11h: Apply reflections, translations, and rotations to figures on the coordinate plane
11g: Identify rigid motions (reflection, translation, rotation) from diagrams
11j: Calculate the surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, pyramids, and cones
11i: Identify and classify three-dimensional figures as polyhedra or non-polyhedra
11k: Interpret orthographic drawings to build models of three-dimensional figures
11l: Use nets to identify solids, find surface area, and construct models
11m: Distinguish between accuracy and precision in measurement contexts
11n: Count the number of significant digits in a given measurement using the rules for significance
12b: Determine whether a given conjecture is true or false
12a: Recognize patterns in numerical, geometric, and real-world sequences and express them as conjectures
12q: Find the distance from a point to a line in the coordinate plane
12c: Determine the truth value of compound statements formed by conjunction and disjunction
12d: Identify the hypothesis and conclusion of a conditional statement and write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive
12f: Apply the Law of Detachment and the Law of Syllogism to draw valid conclusions from conditional statements
12e: Distinguish between inductive and deductive reasoning in given arguments
12g: Apply geometric postulates about points, lines, and planes to justify statements
12h: Write two-column proofs using given information, definitions, and properties
12i: Complete two-column proofs by supplying missing statements and reasons
12j: Write two-column proofs to justify angle relationships including congruent supplements, congruent complements, and right angles
12k: Classify angle pairs formed by two lines cut by a transversal as alternate interior, alternate exterior, corresponding, or consecutive interior angles
12l: Use properties of parallel lines and transversals to find unknown angle measures
12m: Calculate the slope of a line through two given points and interpret its meaning
12n: Write equations of lines in slope-intercept form that are parallel or perpendicular to a given line and pass through a given point
12p: Apply parallel line theorems to solve real-world and geometric problems
12o: Use angle relationships formed by a transversal to prove that two lines are parallel
13b: Determine the coordinates of the image of a point after a reflection in a horizontal or vertical line
13a: Graph the image of a figure after a reflection over a given line on the coordinate plane
13c: Determine whether a given transformation between two figures is a translation and justify the answer
13d: Find the translation vector that maps a preimage to its image and apply it
13f: Analyze properties preserved under rotations and identify common errors in reasoning about rotations
13e: Graph the image of a polygon after a rotation of 90°, 180°, or 270° about a given point on the coordinate plane
13g: Graph the image of a figure after a glide reflection or other composition of transformations on the coordinate plane
13h: Identify a single transformation that is equivalent to a composition of two reflections
13i: Determine whether a regular polygon will tessellate the plane by examining its interior angle measure
13j: Determine whether a tessellation can be created from a given figure and describe the transformation(s) used
13l: Determine whether a figure has rotational symmetry and justify the answer
13k: Identify and draw lines of symmetry for two-dimensional figures
14a: Apply the Triangle Angle-Sum Theorem to find unknown interior angle measures
14b: Use the Exterior Angle Theorem to relate exterior and remote interior angles
14c: Identify all congruent corresponding parts of polygons and write correct congruence statements
14d: Write two-column proofs and paragraph proofs to show that two triangles are congruent
14f: Identify the included angle and apply the SAS Congruence Postulate correctly
14e: Write two-column, paragraph, and flow proofs to prove triangle congruence using the SSS and SAS postulates
14h: Use properties of parallel lines, midpoints, and angle bisectors to find congruent parts needed for ASA or AAS proofs
14g: Write two-column, paragraph, and flow proofs to prove triangle congruence using the ASA and AAS theorems
14i: Apply the Leg-Leg (LL), Hypotenuse-Angle (HA), Leg-Angle (LA), and Hypotenuse-Leg (HL) theorems to prove right triangles congruent
14j: Write two-column proofs and paragraph proofs for right triangle congruence in real-world contexts
14l: Solve for unknown variables using properties of isosceles and equilateral triangles
14k: Write two-column proofs using the Isosceles Triangle Theorem and its converse
14m: Position and label triangles on the coordinate plane using given side lengths and properties
14n: Write coordinate proofs for geometric statements about triangles
2a: Translate verbal sentences and real-world situations into algebraic equations and formulas
2c: Solve one-step equations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division using the Properties of Equality
2e: Solve multi-step linear equations using properties of equality and check solutions by substitution
2g: Solve linear equations that have the variable on both sides of the equation, including those with fractions and grouping symbols
2j: Graph the solution set of an absolute value equation on a number line and write absolute value equations from graphs
2i: Solve absolute value equations of the form
2k: Solve proportions algebraically, including those with variable expressions in numerators and denominators
2m: Solve literal equations and formulas for a specified variable using inverse operations
3a: Express a relation as a table, a graph, a mapping, and a set of ordered pairs and identify the domain and range
3c: Determine whether a relation represented as a graph, table, mapping, set of ordered pairs, or equation is a function
3e: Determine whether a function represented by a graph, equation, table, or real-world situation is discrete, continuous, or neither
3g: Estimate and interpret the x- and y-intercepts of a function from its graph and describe where the function is positive and negative
3i: Identify line symmetry in the graph of a function and determine the intervals where a function is increasing, decreasing, or constant
3k: Sketch graphs of real-world functions from verbal descriptions, identifying intercepts, intervals of increase and decrease, end behavior, extrema, and symmetry
4a: Graph linear equations by making a table of values and by finding and plotting the x- and y-intercepts
4c: Calculate the rate of change from tables, graphs, and real-world data and compute the slope of a line passing through two given points
4e: Interpret slope and rate of change in real-world contexts including grade, elevation, and positioning
4f: Write linear equations in slope-intercept form given the slope and y-intercept and rewrite linear equations from standard form into slope-intercept form
4h: Describe translations, dilations, and reflections of linear functions in relation to the parent function f(x) = x
4j: Determine whether a given sequence is arithmetic and justify the reasoning using the common difference
4l: Graph piecewise-defined functions and step functions (greatest integer functions) and identify their domain and range
4n: Describe translations, dilations, and reflections of absolute value functions from their equations and graph absolute value functions
5a: Write linear equations in slope-intercept form given a point and a slope and given two points
5c: Write linear equations in point-slope form given a point and a slope or two points and convert equations between point-slope, slope-intercept, and standard forms
5e: Analyze scatter plots to determine whether they show positive, negative, or no correlation and interpret the meaning in context
5g: Distinguish between correlation and causation in real-world contexts and explain reasoning for whether a relationship is merely correlated or causal, identifying possible lurking variables
5h: Use technology to calculate the equation of a best-fit line and the correlation coefficient for a bivariate data set and interpret the correlation coefficient r in context
5j: Find the inverse of a relation given its graph, table, or set of ordered pairs and find the inverse of a linear function algebraically using the switch-and-solve method
6a: Graph the solution set of a one-step inequality on a number line and write an inequality that matches a given number-line graph
6c: Solve multi-step linear inequalities using the Distributive Property, combining like terms, and inverse operations
6e: Solve compound inequalities involving "and" and "or" and express the solution algebraically
6g: Solve absolute value inequalities and rewrite them as compound inequalities using and or or
6i: Graph linear inequalities in two variables written in slope-intercept and standard form and write linear inequalities to model real-world constraints
6k: Write systems of inequalities that correspond to a given graph and determine whether an ordered pair is a solution to a system without graphing
7a: Graph systems of linear equations and determine the number of solutions; classify systems as consistent or inconsistent and as independent or dependent
7c: Solve systems of linear equations using the substitution method
7e: Solve systems of linear equations using elimination by addition or subtraction and recognize when adding or subtracting equations will eliminate a variable
7g: Solve systems of linear equations using elimination when multiplication of one or both equations is required
7i: Graph systems of linear inequalities and identify the overlapping solution region (feasible region)
7k: Solve real-world optimization problems by defining variables, writing a system of inequalities, graphing, and interpreting the feasible region
8a: Identify exponential behavior from data using common ratios
8c: Describe transformations of exponential functions verbally and in function notation
8d: Write equations for transformed exponential functions given a graph
8f: Apply the compound-interest formula to investment and depreciation problems
8e: Write an exponential function given two points on its graph
8g: Rewrite exponential functions so rates share the same time unit for direct comparison
8h: Determine whether a given sequence is geometric by checking for a constant common ratio
8i: Write and use explicit formulas to model real-world situations involving exponential growth and decay
8k: Convert between recursive and explicit formulas for arithmetic and geometric sequences
8j: Find the first several terms of a sequence given a recursive formula
9a: Calculate the mean, median, and mode of a data set
9c: Analyze data displays to draw conclusions and answer questions about a data set
9b: Construct dot plots, bar graphs, and histograms to represent data sets
9d: Identify sample bias in survey and sampling methods and explain how it affects results
9e: Recognize biased survey questions and identify whose interests they may serve
9f: Calculate the range, interquartile range, and standard deviation of a data set
9g: Determine the five-number summary and construct a box plot from a data set
9i: Choose appropriate measures of center and spread based on whether a distribution is symmetric or skewed
9h: Construct and interpret histograms and box plots to visualize the shape of a data distribution
9k: Construct and interpret box plots and histograms to compare the shape, center, and spread of two data sets
9j: Describe how adding or multiplying every value in a data set affects the mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation
9l: Construct and complete two-way frequency tables from verbal descriptions or given data
